name: canonical variates
best_quote: The aℓ are referred to as discriminant coordinates, not to be confused with discriminant functions. They are also referred to as canonical variates, since an alternative derivation of these results is through a canonical correlation analysis of the indicator response matrix Y on the predictor matrix X.

name: adjusted response
best_quote: In the second and third line we have re-expressed the Newton step as a weighted least squares step, with the response z = Xβ^old + W^(-1)(y − p) sometimes known as the adjusted response.

name: support vector machine
best_quote: In Chapter 12 we discuss a more attractive alternative known as the support vector machine, which allows for overlap, but minimizes a measure of the extent of this overlap.

name: warm start
best_quote: Then λ is decreased again and the process is repeated, using the previous solution as a 'warm start' for the new value of λ.

name: cholesky decomposition
best_quote: Least squares fitting is usually done via the Cholesky decomposition of the matrix XT X or a QR decomposition of X. With N observations and p features, the Cholesky decomposition requires p3+Np2/2 operations

name: indicator response matrix
best_quote: Here each of the response categories are coded via an indicator variable. Thus if G has K classes, there will be K such indicators Yk, k = 1, . . . , K, with Yk = 1 if G = k else 0.

name: masking in classification
best_quote: The three regression lines (left panel) are included, and we see that the line corresponding to the middle class is horizontal and its fitted values are never dominant! Thus, observations from class 2 are classified either as class 1 or class 3.

name: class-conditional density
best_quote: Suppose fk(x) is the class-conditional density of X in class G = k, and let πk be the prior probability of class k

name: linear discriminant function
best_quote: From (4.9) we see that the linear discriminant functions δk(x) = x^T Σ^(-1)μk - (1/2)μk^T Σ^(-1)μk + log πk are an equivalent description of the decision rule

name: pooled covariance matrix
best_quote: where Σ̂ is the pooled covariance matrix as used in LDA.

name: quadratic discriminant function
best_quote: if the Σk are not assumed to be equal, then the convenient cancellations in (4.9) do not occur; in particular the pieces quadratic in x remain. We then get quadratic discriminant functions (QDA)

name: sphering the data
best_quote: Sphere the data with respect to the common covariance estimate Σ̂: X* ← D^(-1/2) U^T X, where Σ̂ = UDU^T. The common covariance estimate of X* will now be the identity.

name: reduced-rank lda
best_quote: The K centroids in p-dimensional input space lie in an affine subspace of dimension ≤ K − 1, and if p is much larger than K, this will be a considerable drop in dimension.

name: fisher's linear discriminant
best_quote: Find the linear combination Z = a^T X such that the between-class variance is maximized relative to the within-class variance.

name: rayleigh quotient
best_quote: Fisher's problem therefore amounts to maximizing the Rayleigh quotient, max_a a^T Ba / a^T Wa

name: discriminant coordinates
best_quote: The aℓ are referred to as discriminant coordinates, not to be confused with discriminant functions.

name: between-class covariance
best_quote: compute B*, the covariance matrix of M* (B for between-class covariance)

name: within-class covariance
best_quote: compute the K × p matrix of class centroids M and the common covariance matrix W (for within-class covariance)

name: logit transformation
best_quote: Here the monotone transformation is the logit transformation: log[p/(1−p)]

name: log-odds
best_quote: The model is specified in terms of K − 1 log-odds or logit transformations (reflecting the constraint that the probabilities sum to one).

name: score equations
best_quote: To maximize the log-likelihood, we set its derivatives to zero. These score equations are ∂ℓ(β)/∂β = X^T(y − p(x;β)) = 0

name: hessian matrix
best_quote: To solve the score equations (4.21), we use the Newton–Raphson algorithm, which requires the second-derivative or Hessian matrix

name: iteratively reweighted least squares
best_quote: These equations get solved repeatedly, since at each iteration p changes, and hence so does W and z. This algorithm is referred to as iteratively reweighted least squares or IRLS

name: adjusted response in logistic regression
best_quote: In the second and third line we have re-expressed the Newton step as a weighted least squares step, with the response z = Xβ^old + W^(-1)(y − p) sometimes known as the adjusted response.

name: wald test
best_quote: Each of these correspond formally to a test of the null hypothesis that the coefficient in question is zero, while all the others are not (also known as the Wald test).

name: residual deviance
best_quote: The residual deviance of a fitted model is minus twice its log-likelihood, and the deviance between two models is the difference of their individual residual deviances

name: pearson chi-square statistic
best_quote: The weighted residual sum-of-squares is the familiar Pearson chi-square statistic

name: rao score test
best_quote: Popular shortcuts are the Rao score test which tests for inclusion of a term, and the Wald test which can be used to test for exclusion of a term.

name: l1 regularized logistic regression
best_quote: The L1 penalty used in the lasso (Section 3.4.2) can be used for variable selection and shrinkage with any linear regression model. For logistic regression, we would maximize a penalized version

name: conditional likelihood
best_quote: The logistic regression model leaves the marginal density of X as an arbitrary density function Pr(X), and fits the parameters of Pr(G|X) by maximizing the conditional likelihood

name: joint likelihood in lda
best_quote: With LDA we fit the parameters by maximizing the full log-likelihood, based on the joint density Pr(X, G) = Pr(X)Pr(G|X) = φ(X;μk,Σ)πk

name: separating hyperplane
best_quote: We have seen that linear discriminant analysis and logistic regression both estimate linear decision boundaries in similar but slightly different ways. For the rest of this chapter we describe separating hyperplane classifiers.

name: perceptron
best_quote: Classifiers such as (4.39), that compute a linear combination of the input features and return the sign, were called perceptrons in the engineering literature in the late 1950s (Rosenblatt, 1958).

name: affine set
best_quote: Figure 4.15 depicts a hyperplane or affine set L defined by the equation f(x) = β₀ + β^T x = 0

name: signed distance to hyperplane
best_quote: The signed distance of any point x to L is given by β^T(x − x₀)/||β|| = f(x)/||β||

name: perceptron learning algorithm
best_quote: The perceptron learning algorithm tries to find a separating hyperplane by minimizing the distance of misclassified points to the decision boundary.

name: stochastic gradient descent
best_quote: The algorithm in fact uses stochastic gradient descent to minimize this piecewise linear criterion. This means that rather than computing the sum of the gradient contributions of each observation followed by a step in the negative gradient direction, a step is taken after each observation is visited.

name: lagrange primal function
best_quote: The Lagrange (primal) function, to be minimized w.r.t. β and β₀, is LP(β,β₀) = (1/2)||β||² − Σᵢαᵢ[yᵢ(xᵢᵀβ + β₀) − 1]

name: support points in separating hyperplanes
best_quote: From (4.50) we see that the solution vector β is defined in terms of a linear combination of the support points xᵢ—those points defined to be on the boundary of the slab via αᵢ > 0.

name: piecewise polynomial
best_quote: A piecewise polynomial function f(X) is obtained by dividing the domain of X into contiguous intervals, and representing f by a separate polynomial in each interval.

name: knots in splines
best_quote: A piecewise polynomial function f(X) is obtained by dividing the domain of X into contiguous intervals, and representing f by a separate polynomial in each interval.

name: continuity constraints in splines
best_quote: Except in special cases, we would typically prefer the third panel, which is also piecewise linear, but restricted to be continuous at the two knots. These continuity restrictions lead to linear constraints on the parameters

name: regression splines
best_quote: These fixed-knot splines are also known as regression splines. One needs to select the order of the spline, the number of knots and their placement.

name: smoother matrix
best_quote: Again the fit is linear in y, and the finite linear operator Sλ is known as the smoother matrix. One consequence of this linearity is that the recipe for producing f̂ from y does not depend on y itself; Sλ depends only on the xi and λ.

name: penalty matrix
best_quote: K is known as the penalty matrix, and indeed a quadratic form in K has a representation in terms of a weighted sum of squared (divided) second differences.

name: shrinking smoothers
best_quote: either left alone, or shrunk to zero—that is, a projection matrix such as Hξ above has M eigenvalues equal to 1, and the rest are 0. For this reason smoothing splines are referred to as shrinking smoothers, while regression splines are projection smoothers

name: B-splines
best_quote: The B-spline basis, described in the Appendix to this chapter, allows for efficient computations even when the number of knots K is large.

name: Haar basis functions
best_quote: for i = 1, . . . , K + 2M −1. These are also known as Haar basis functions.

name: roughness penalty
best_quote: the second term penalizes curvature in the function

name: feature extraction
best_quote: Preprocessing of high-dimensional features is a very general and powerful method for improving the performance of a learning algorithm.

name: linear smoother
best_quote: A smoothing spline with prechosen λ is an example of a linear smoother (as in linear operator). This is because the estimated parameters in (5.12) are a linear combination of the yᵢ.

name: hat matrix
best_quote: Here the linear operator Hξ is a projection operator, also known as the hat matrix in statistics.

name: effective degrees of freedom
best_quote: By analogy we define the effective degrees of freedom... This very useful definition allows us a more intuitive way to parameterize the smoothing spline, and indeed many other smoothers as well, in a consistent fashion.

name: truncated power basis
best_quote: A more direct way to proceed in this case is to use a basis that incorporates the constraints: h₁(X) = 1, h₂(X) = X, h₃(X) = (X − ξ₁)₊, h₄(X) = (X − ξ₂)₊

name: natural cubic spline
best_quote: A natural cubic spline adds additional constraints, namely that the function is linear beyond the boundary knots. This frees up four degrees of freedom (two constraints each in both boundary regions), which can be spent more profitably by sprinkling more knots in the interior region.

name: order-M spline
best_quote: More generally, an order-M spline with knots ξj, j = 1, . . . , K is a piecewise-polynomial of order M, and has continuous derivatives up to order M − 2.

name: cubic spline
best_quote: It is claimed that cubic splines are the lowest-order spline for which the knot-discontinuity is not visible to the human eye.

name: pointwise variance
best_quote: This can be conveniently summarized in terms of the pointwise variance of spline functions fit by least squares.

name: backward stepwise deletion
best_quote: We carried out a backward stepwise deletion process, dropping terms from this model while preserving the group structure of each term, rather than dropping one coefficient at a time.

name: functional modeling
best_quote: In this example we use splines to reduce flexibility rather than increase it; the application comes under the general heading of functional modeling.

name: contrast functional
best_quote: The coefficients compute a contrast functional, and will have appreciable values in regions of frequency where the log-periodograms differ between the two classes.

name: filtered features
best_quote: These filtered versions of the features were then used as inputs into a learning procedure

name: smoothing spline
best_quote: Here we discuss a spline basis method that avoids the knot selection problem completely by using a maximal set of knots. The complexity of the fit is controlled by regularization.

name: penalized residual sum of squares
best_quote: among all functions f(x) with two continuous derivatives, find one that minimizes the penalized residual sum of squares... The first term measures closeness to the data, while the second term penalizes curvature in the function, and λ establishes a tradeoff between the two.

name: Sobolev space
best_quote: The criterion (5.9) is defined on an infinite-dimensional function space—in fact, a Sobolev space of functions for which the second term is defined.

name: generalized ridge regression
best_quote: The solution is easily seen to be θ̂ = (NᵀN + λΩₙ)⁻¹Nᵀy, a generalized ridge regression.

name: Reinsch form
best_quote: it is convenient to rewrite Sλ in the Reinsch form Sλ = (I + λK)⁻¹, where K does not depend on λ.

name: projection smoothers
best_quote: a projection matrix such as Hξ above has M eigenvalues equal to 1, and the rest are 0. For this reason smoothing splines are referred to as shrinking smoothers, while regression splines are projection smoothers

name: Demmler-Reinsch basis
best_quote: One can reparametrize the smoothing spline using the basis vectors uₖ (the Demmler–Reinsch basis).

name: integrated squared prediction error
best_quote: The integrated squared prediction error (EPE) combines both bias and variance in a single summary... Note that this is averaged both over the training sample (giving rise to f̂λ), and the values of the (independently chosen) prediction points (X, Y).

name: nonparametric logistic regression
best_quote: The smoothing spline problem (5.9) in Section 5.4 is posed in a regression setting. It is typically straightforward to transfer this technology to other domains. Here we consider logistic regression with a single quantitative input X.

name: penalized log-likelihood
best_quote: We construct the penalized log-likelihood criterion

name: weighted smoothing spline
best_quote: Referring back to (5.12) and (5.14), we see that the update fits a weighted smoothing spline to the working response z

name: tensor product basis
best_quote: Then the M₁ × M₂ dimensional tensor product basis defined by... can be used for representing a two-dimensional function

name: wavelet transform
best_quote: for signal or image recognition a popular approach is to first transform the raw features via a wavelet transform x* = Hᵀx (Section 5.9)

name: AIC statistic
best_quote: The AIC statistic (Section 7.5) was used to drop terms, and all the terms remaining in the final model would cause AIC to increase if deleted from the model

name: B-spline basis
best_quote: The B-spline basis, described in the Appendix to this chapter, allows for efficient computations even when the number of knots K is large.

name: boundary knots
best_quote: The polynomials fit beyond the boundary knots behave even more wildly than the corresponding global polynomials in that region.

name: interior knots
best_quote: bs(x,df=7) in R generates a basis matrix of cubic-spline functions evaluated at the N observations in x, with the 7−3 = 4 interior knots

name: idempotent matrix
best_quote: Hξ Hξ = Hξ (idempotent), while Sλ Sλ ⪯ Sλ, meaning that the right-hand side exceeds the left-hand side by a positive semidefinite matrix.

name: positive semidefinite matrix
best_quote: Both are symmetric, positive semidefinite matrices.

name: eigendecomposition
best_quote: Since Sλ is symmetric (and positive semidefinite), it has a real eigendecomposition.

name: MARS
best_quote: The MARS procedure in Chapter 9 uses a greedy algorithm with some additional approximations to achieve a practical compromise.

name: equivalent kernel
best_quote: These weights li(x0) combine the weighting kernel Kλ(x0, ·) and the least squares operations, and are sometimes referred to as the equivalent kernel.

name: expected prediction error
best_quote: A related quantity is the expected prediction error (or expected test error) where this expectation averages over everything that is random, including the randomness in the training set that produced f̂.

name: training error
best_quote: Training error is not a good estimate of the test error. Training error consistently decreases with model complexity, typically dropping to zero if we increase the model complexity enough.

name: local linear regression
best_quote: Locally weighted regression solves a separate weighted least squares problem at each target point x0

name: bias-variance decomposition
best_quote: Err(x₀) = σ²_ε + [Ef̂(x₀) - f(x₀)]² + E[f̂(x₀) - Ef̂(x₀)]². The first term is the variance of the target around its true mean f(x₀), and cannot be avoided no matter how well we estimate f(x₀). The second term is the squared bias, the amount by which the average of our estimate differs from the true mean; the last term is the variance

name: in-sample error
best_quote: The nature of the optimism in err is easiest to understand when we focus instead on the in-sample error Err_in = (1/N)Σᵢ E_Y⁰[L(Yᵢ⁰, f̂(xᵢ))|T]

name: cp statistic
best_quote: Using expression (7.24), applicable when d parameters are fit under squared error loss, leads to a version of the so-called Cₚ statistic: Ĉₚ = err + 2(d/N)σ̂²_ε

name: automatic kernel carpentry
best_quote: Local linear regression automatically modifies the kernel to correct the bias exactly to first order, a phenomenon dubbed as automatic kernel carpentry.

name: local polynomial regression
best_quote: We can fit local polynomial fits of any degree d, with solution f̂(x0) = α̂(x0) + Σⱼ₌₁ᵈ β̂ⱼ(x0)xⱼ₀. In fact, an expansion such as (6.10) will tell us that the bias will only have components of degree d+1 and higher

name: trimming the hills and filling the valleys
best_quote: Local linear fits tend to be biased in regions of curvature of the true function, a phenomenon referred to as trimming the hills and filling the valleys.

name: nadaraya-watson kernel smoother
best_quote: The Nadaraya–Watson kernel smoother fits a constant locally with weights supplied by a p-dimensional kernel.

name: varying coefficient models
best_quote: For given Z, this is a linear model, but each of the coefficients can vary with Z. It is natural to fit such a model by locally weighted least squares

name: local likelihood
best_quote: Local likelihood allows a relaxation from a globally linear model to one that is locally linear.

name: parzen density estimate
best_quote: The Parzen density estimate is preferred because it counts observations close to x0 with weights that decrease with distance from x0.

name: naive bayes classifier
best_quote: The naive Bayes model assumes that given a class G = j, the features Xk are independent. Despite these rather optimistic assumptions, naive Bayes classifiers often outperform far more sophisticated alternatives.

name: radial basis functions
best_quote: Radial basis functions combine these ideas, by treating the kernel functions Kλ(ξ, x) as basis functions.

name: rbf network
best_quote: This model is commonly referred to as an RBF network, an alternative to the sigmoidal neural network discussed in Chapter 11; the ξⱼ and λⱼ playing the role of the weights. This criterion is nonconvex with multiple local minima

name: renormalized radial basis functions
best_quote: Renormalized radial basis functions hⱼ(x) = D(||x-ξⱼ||/λ) / Σₖ D(||x-ξₖ||/λ) avoid this problem.

name: gaussian mixture model
best_quote: The Gaussian mixture model has the form f(x) = Σₘ αₘ φ(x; μₘ, Σₘ) with mixing proportions αₘ, Σₘ αₘ = 1, and each Gaussian density has a mean μₘ and covariance matrix Σₘ.

name: memory-based methods
best_quote: Kernel and local regression and density estimation are memory-based methods: the model is the entire training data set, and the fitting is done at evaluation or prediction time.

name: model selection
best_quote: Model selection: estimating the performance of different models in order to choose the best one.

name: model assessment
best_quote: Model assessment: having chosen a final model, estimating its prediction error (generalization error) on new data.

name: model bias
best_quote: The first term on the right-hand side is the average squared model bias, the error between the best-fitting linear approximation and the true function.

name: optimism
best_quote: We define the optimism as the difference between Err_in and the training error err. The amount by which err underestimates the true error depends on how strongly yᵢ affects its own prediction.

name: effective number of parameters
best_quote: The effective number of parameters is defined as df(S) = trace(S), the sum of the diagonal elements of S (also known as the effective degrees-of-freedom).

name: vapnik-chervonenkis dimension
best_quote: The VC dimension of the class {f(x,α)} is defined to be the largest number of points (in some configuration) that can be shattered by members of {f(x,α)}. A set of points is said to be shattered by a class of functions if, no matter how we assign a binary label to each point, a member of the class can perfectly separate them.

name: structural risk minimization
best_quote: Vapnik's structural risk minimization (SRM) approach fits a nested sequence of models of increasing VC dimensions h₁ < h₂ < ··· , and then chooses the model with the smallest value of the upper bound.

name: k-fold cross-validation
best_quote: We split the data into K roughly equal-sized parts; for the kth part, we fit the model to the other K−1 parts of the data, and calculate the prediction error of the fitted model when predicting the kth part of the data. We do this for k=1,2,...,K and combine the K estimates of prediction error.

name: one-standard-error rule
best_quote: Often a 'one-standard error' rule is used with cross-validation, in which we choose the most parsimonious model whose error is no more than one standard error above the error of the best model.

name: epanechnikov kernel
best_quote: The Epanechnikov kernel has compact support (needed when used with nearest-neighbor window size). Another popular compact kernel is based on the tri-cube function

name: tri-cube kernel
best_quote: Another popular compact kernel is based on the tri-cube function. This is flatter on the top (like the nearest-neighbor box) and is differentiable at the boundary of its support.

name: boundary bias in kernel methods
best_quote: Locally-weighted averages can be badly biased on the boundaries of the domain, because of the asymmetry of the kernel in that region. By fitting straight lines rather than constants locally, we can remove this bias exactly to first order

name: curse of dimensionality in local regression
best_quote: One of the manifestations of the curse of dimensionality is that the fraction of points close to the boundary increases to one as the dimension grows. It is impossible to simultaneously maintain localness (⇒low bias) and a sizable sample in the neighborhood (⇒low variance) as the dimension increases, without the total sample size increasing exponentially in p.

name: anova decomposition in regression
best_quote: It is natural to consider analysis-of-variance (ANOVA) decompositions of the form f(X) = α + Σⱼ gⱼ(Xⱼ) + Σⱼ<ₖ gⱼₖ(Xⱼ,Xₖ) + ··· and then introduce structure by eliminating some of the higher-order terms.

name: trellis display
best_quote: The trellis display here shows ozone as a function of radiation, conditioned on the other two variables, temperature and wind speed. Above each of the panels is an indication of the range of values present in that panel for each of the conditioning values.

name: structured kernels
best_quote: A more general approach is to use a positive semidefinite matrix A to weigh the different coordinates: Kλ,A(x₀,x) = D(||(x-x₀)ᵀA||/λ). Entire coordinates or directions can be downgraded or omitted by imposing appropriate restrictions on A.

name: instantaneous prefix code
best_quote: This code is known as an instantaneous prefix code: no code is the prefix of any other, and the receiver (who knows all of the possible codes), knows exactly when the message has been completely sent.

name: learning curve
best_quote: Figure 7.8 shows a hypothetical 'learning curve' for a classifier on a given task, a plot of 1−Err versus the size of the training set N. The performance of the classifier improves as the training set size increases
